speaker: interviewer
text: Ingber on September 16, 1996. Just to go back a little bit, when you had to go to the ghetto, what could you bring with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the both hands, whatever it's possible to bring, whatever it's possible to take. So I took a kettle from the stove. And I went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: With just the clothes on your back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you just went out. And my mother closed the door. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- when, when did they give-- when they gave you the order that you are to move into the ghetto, how much time did they give you to prepare?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't rem-- I don't think so they gave the time, no. Came in, out, out, out, and that's it. So whatever is possible in the both, whatever you can take. And I took a kettle. And I went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave the order that you had to move to the ghetto? Who came to tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ukrainian and German police-- everybody was marching, young fellows, out, out, out. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they treat you? Were they very, were they very rude to you? Were they polite? Were they distant?

speaker: interviewee
text: March, march, faster, faster. And that's it. And we had to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they hit anyone at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who remembers? Probably, they didn't-- they didn't kiss us-- [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: --for sure not. How long did you stay in the Luck ghetto for?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a good couple of months, for a couple of months. And then it started the shooting. They, you know-- they actually do to kill. So and I run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Aktion to kill-- how were they shooting people, in the street like that? Or were they calling them together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they to-- I don't know, but they told me that they took them in a one space and put them on trucks. And they send it 20 miles from Luck. It's called [POLISH]. I have a picture. I was looking. I have a picture from the monument which we put. This was 20 kilometers after Luck. And then they killed them. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you started hearing about these Aktions taking place, what was the thought--

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't hear. It came suddenly. There was no radio, no news. It came. They said to come on this and this place. And everybody out from the houses, and they went. And they checked each house, you know, to find, you know. And they took them out. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever come to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I ran away. I run out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what made you-- why, why did you run out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. My mother said, you go. You look like go. And I went. And I don't know how, but I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your mother decided not to go with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No I did-- she didn't even think about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't think to go. Who, who thought, thought young people were thinking? In this time, 39 years old, a woman is already not a young person. Here, it's, it's a teenager. But in Europe, was not.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she told you to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I run away. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your brother? She didn't tell him to go with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was not home.

speaker: interviewer
text: The younger brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: The younger brother also, because he went to work, to-- there was a camp not far from our house. So he went to work. And then he never came back. And then they thought that [? she ?] he went away somewhere. And in the camp, he froze his toes. Anyway, he-- where and what, I have no idea. What was-- what happened to him, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were alone with your mother when she told you to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, so I didn't say goodbye, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just--

speaker: interviewee
text: And she didn't say goodbye. I just run out. And I left. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did you have with you when you left? Did you have anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The way I was dressed, the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any money with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any money with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you ran out of the ghetto. How did you manage to escape from the ghetto? How did you leave so that the soldiers didn't see you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Simple-- they like me, too-- because I didn't have no arm, no, no-- the, this. And I didn't look like a Jewish girl. And it wasn't time. It was dark. And I don't know. I tell you the truth, was-- it's a miracle or not a miracle. I don't know what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: The fact I'm here-- that's all. This what I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you left the ghetto. It was nighttime. And you went out into the city of Luck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go? Were you running? Were you walking? How--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I went-- I don't-- this, I can't. I tried to figure out how it was after the-- your interview. And I couldn't take it, you know, to combine one with the other, how it was. But I know that I landed once at a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] at a Ukrainian family, which they used to live near us. His sister lived in our house a long, long time ago. And [? they ?] were never, you know, like evangelist. So I was there for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: You--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he kept me.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in, in a basement, in a cellar where he had the pigs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were pigs living there when you were living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. It was a few pigs there. I was there in. And he-- I came in. Anyway, he sent me down. I didn't know. And then I came down. There was a Jewish boy, a little boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yankele-- this I know. He told me. He was, I think, seven years old. And it was like, you-- you know-- from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from wood, made, you know, like, like a bed. Like, it's not a bed-- you know, like a place where you keep all kind of things. So the boy was there, the little boy. You know, he was there. He said, here is another boy. Then in the house, he had a family-- I think a couple.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish couple-- I don't know who it is, because I never met them. And then one day, I was there in the basement. And I hear some noises upstairs. But we couldn't-- me and the, the little boy. And then he came up. And he run out and said, run away. Because they got the Jewish family in my house. They took away. So they might going to go downstairs to look.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- the two of us run away. I don't know where this little boy went away. And I went. So then a Polish lady met me walking. Somebody-- somebody you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't know her. But she recognized, you know, on my face that see how I look and all this. So she said, don't go. Because I had to pass bridge. So she said, don't go. Because the German police are there. You come with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she took-- come with me? What's the difference? Another hour to live-- I didn't care. So and she took into-- I don't know, is it a brother or a brother-in-law, I can't remember. And she took me into him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they kept me there. They let in. And it was in a hallway when you walk in. It was a hallway, and like, you know, for chickens, you know, a place. So they open the pieces. It was not a door but just a few boards.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said, can you crawl in? It was a table. And they said, can you crawl in? So like this, and this is the way-- the position I was in lying there. You couldn't sit up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Couldn't, because it was no place. It was very narrow, you know, from chickens. And I was sitting there. So then the men came in. And they said they're going to keep me. But I had to become a Catholic. I said, no problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they start to teach me and gave me, you know, all kind of [INAUDIBLE]-- you know, with books, prayer books, small one I should read and learn and to remember. But they used to give me food. What-- what kind of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they used to. What kind, I don't know. I don't know. I would eat anything. And so I-- I was not fussy. So and then after a while, I was there. So he said he wants I should become a Catholic. And he going to take me to the church and the priest will-- I said OK. He's not afraid. I'm not afraid. I-- anyway, I'm already. My-- I know what's [? going to wait ?] for me, is they're going to kill me anyway. I have nothing to lose. So-- but then he said, no. Somebody is afraid-- he's afraid in case if somebody will see me or this or that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, they took me in into the house and near the Virgin Mary, you know? They put some water on me and everything. OK, I'm a Catholic. And [INAUDIBLE] he said in the morning and at night, you pray. Then a couple times, they called me in. And they heard how I pray without-- the prayer. And I knew and everything. And it was good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he came. He said, no, you have to go. Because somebody noticed you. I never stepped out. And nobody was able to notice me. But he said somebody heard me.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were-- how did you study for your-- for your-- for your Catholic baptism if you're inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: But this is into his house-- he took me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were staying underneath-- underneath in the hallway there. You were staying in that little place.

words.text: You were staying in that little place.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, in his room, where it was a, a nice picture of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so you were staying in a room afterwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in the room-- they made a whole celebration.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and that's it. And then he came. And he said, yeah, you have to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about that he baptized you? You didn't-- how did you feel about? Did you feel you were giving up being Jewish? Or you didn't take it seriously?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who feels? I didn't feel anything. I was like a piece of wood. I didn't feel anything. I didn't think I didn't feel. I just know that soon, they're going to kill me. This is my thing, my dreaming and everything. So I didn't-- at that time, you don't think anything when you're in a moment like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then he came after a while. And he said, I-- it was not bad. It was very good there. But then he said, no. So they gave me, you know, a basket on my hair-- with a, a shawl-- you know, like a peasant. They gave me all to get dressed and some milk, a bottle, and a bread. And then he said, you know what? They're in there, I notice. I think they are some Jews there, not far from this house. So and I went there. You go try. So I have nothing to lose. So I came. I came. So the lady saw me. I knocked the door. And there was a big dog. But He didn't touch me. And she came out. And she said-- and I told her that I am a Catholic. I told already this way. Because this is the way he told me to say. I'm a [? good ?] Catholic and this and that. And can I stay with you? So she right away, you know, start to cross. What I going to do? What I going to do? But she said, OK. Go into the cellar. Outside, she had a cellar where she kept beets and potatoes. And I was winter. Of what-- of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I slept on the potatoes without any blanket, without any [INAUDIBLE]. I didn't freeze. It was warm enough. It was cold, but I didn't feel anything. And then after, I think, two days or three days, she said, come up. And she put a stepladder, I should go on the attic. And then, on the attic was a Jewish family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Guttman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was this woman's name whose house you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: The woman whose house you lived in, what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Segova, Segova-- [? Sega. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she a young woman, an elderly woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, an old woman, a old, wide woman with big eyes and-- so and she said, you stay with, stay with the people. The people were not happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish people-- why not? Because one more person to eat, whatever it is, the bread. Let's say they have to share for six people. They're going to have to share another piece. They have to give up from this. But they had no choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were seven of you in the attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was in the attic. I went with the stepladder. They put a stepladder. I went. And I was there till the 4th of February, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this happened-- when did you start-- when did you go into the attic? When? What month and--

speaker: interviewee
text: After a couple of day-- I don't know the dates. I didn't know the months. Don't ask me. I didn't know the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said it was winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I just-- it was cold. I just know in the morning when the, the chicken, the rooster used to start. So I said, oh, it's 3 o'clock in the morning, I knew. I didn't have any watches. I didn't know anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said it was 1942 when you started?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- yeah. And this, in 1944, the 4 of February, she put in the stepladder. And she said, come down. [? Jews ?] are back. So the Russian came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe we can go back a little bit to what life was like in the attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like in the attic-- it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: There were how many pe-- how many people were in the Guttman family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think five-- a daughter and a son and another daughter and the mother. Five, six-- and the mother's sister which went-- [? mother ?] six.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, five-- five, and I was the sixth.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was the attic? What did the attic look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The attics look, look like, like a grave, nothing special. It was no beds or nothing, just a little bit of straw on the bottom. And this is the way we used to sleep. They had some blankets, which-- because they, the Jewish family, paid in advance to those people. You know, they thought it's going to be for a week or for two weeks. They didn't think that it's going to be for-- you know, the Polish people, they didn't think it's going to last. So they stayed there. So sometimes, they let me put the-- my toes a little bit under the blanket and sometimes not. And I was without anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were just wearing the dress that you had left the ghetto with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: One dress?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have a chance to wash the dress?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oof, what I going to wear? I don't say a brassiere or panties. Forget it-- or stockings. Anyway, forget it. I have everything now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about in the attic? What did it-- what did the roof of the attic look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The attic is-- it was, you know, like a-- the roof was like this from straw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any window?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a small, you know, to, to take a look, you know, with a magnifying glass. It was no-- it was dark all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How high was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very-- you had to lie all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: On your back?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the back or straight, makes no difference. Even now, I sleep like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you spent a year and a half just lying in the attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, just lying. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When you, you have to go to the bathroom, there was no bathroom. So there was a pail in which the lady used to, whenever it was very dark and she had the chance, so she used to pick up the pail and to go there somewhere and to spill it out and to gave the pail back we should try for another day. Or sometimes, it has to stay for a week because she didn't have a chance to take it out. It's not like here. You have two bathrooms. And that's it. That's the life.

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- so she would clear out the pail. Why wouldn't she have a chance sometimes to do it in a week?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because some people came. You couldn't move. She couldn't say anything that I have people. And she was also afraid. Because if they would catch us, she would be killed. There is no question about it. She was afraid. She was with her husband. Her husband worked in a auto parts factory, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an older man. They didn't have children. And she kept me. And she said, it's-- she going to take me. Sometimes, she used to take me down, I should fix her some socks for her husband, you know. Because upstairs, you couldn't see anything-- you know, sometimes but very seldom. And that's it. We used to be in the dark and lying and sometimes talking to each other and sometimes no. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you talk about when you talked?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't-- not about a movie, no the TV. [LAUGHS] So will she get a today a potato or not? Who knows if she's going to bring a potato or if she be able to bring a little bit of water. That's it. This, this was the dream, to have a piece of bread and still to leave a piece. And I say, oh, it's going to be enough. This was my dream. And that's it. And thank God for this. I had bread. I have everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about-- how did you know when there were people downstairs visiting her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, how do you-- oh, she had a broomstick. So she used to knock [INAUDIBLE]. So she used to scream, boy, how come-- how they got upstairs? You know, [INAUDIBLE]. So we knew. Nobody even said a word. And nobody moved. Because even you can [? bust. ?] But nobody-- otherwise, we will be dead a long time ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have a chance in that year and a half to go outside at all at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I went two or three times. I went to back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she know that you went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, she let me. Because it was no-- because they-- all of them, they didn't go. So she used to ask me. So I, I think twice or three time. I used to go, got the piece bread, got potato, you know. So not only for me, you know, should be even for the lady also. Because she was a poor lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would go in the back yard or in the back?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- near the house, you know, around, you know, into back. It was a lot of people, they used to go in back. So some of them, they killed. And I was the lucky one, they didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever-- when you went out at night like that-- did you ever hear any soldiers or see any soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was dark. It was all over dark at night. I don't remember even to seeing soldiers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the attic, did you ever talk about Jewish holidays? Or did you not know when they happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knew when-- what date it is? And was I going to talk about holidays? My mother won't come. She won't give me to eat. So what is the difference for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew when it before Christmas, she used to bring up, you know, like a tablet, you know, like the Christian-- they give to swallow. How you call this? Like a bonbon or a wafer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a bonbon, no-- it's like a tablet, like a piece of dough was thin, thin one, like the priest gives to swallow, you know, just--

speaker: interviewer
text: The wafer?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how you call this. So we knew it's Christmas. Or we knew if she gave this, so we're going to have something to eat the next day. That's all. This is the only-- the dream was to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did her husband ever help her with this, with taking care of the people there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he know that you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew-- no, the husband didn't know that I'm there. But he knew that, see, the other people are. This, he knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't she tell him that you were there if she was already hiding five people?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't want, because she-- he going to scream at her. You have not enough. Why you took another one? He said-- when I came down, when the put down the stepladder to go down the 4th of February, so he asked. He said, what kind of a goat is this one? You know, when I step down. That's all. And then they liked me. They wanted I should be with her, to remain. She wanted I should be like her daughter. So I told her, whatever it's going to be possible, I will help you. That's why I didn't go to my house. I right away went away from, from the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you came down-- just a few other questions about what life was like in the attic. You always wore the same dress. Did you ever have a chance-- was there ever any extra water to wash your hair, wash your face?

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't-- it would, would got frozen everything. Because it was very cold. Once, I washed, you know, with a little bit of water. And then I got, you know, like the ice parts from my hair. I didn't at the time, because I cut my hair with a-- a knife. This was, I think, in the-- in the basement when I was by the-- by the man with the, with the Jewish boy. So with a knife, I cut, you know, just a little bit. Because I had long hair. But just the same, you can't keep-- [? but ?] tangled.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you-- when liberation came in 1944, could you describe what you looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: You. What did you look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a skeleton. [LAUGHS] How can I look after so many time, and, and all this. But I look not bad now at this age after 50 years, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were very thin. And your hair-- did you have your hair? Or had it fallen out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, they didn't. I had my hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was-- was it its natural color or?

speaker: interviewee
text: My natural color, sure-- at this time to color? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were very thin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were very thin. Everybody in the family was very thin.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not very thin-- normal, normal. But after so many-- so long, not, you know, not proper eat, not proper getting not proper sleep and not to get washed and everything. But right away, I got on the chubby side, start to eat bread. And I couldn't finish a bread. I finished. And then I still want some more.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- how did you feel when she told you that you can come down from the attic for the last time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were happy and everything. Then I start to cry. I didn't cry all the time. Then I start to cry. What I going to do? Where I going to go? And to who I going to go? And what is going to be with me? In there, the family wanted I should go with them. And I didn't want, because I had them enough in the attic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe we take a little break now?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, I want to get [LAUGHS] finished.
